steps.n: This is the basic negative particle that makes what comes after it negative. In this ayah, it negates the verb أُقْسِمُ (I swear).
Added fatha (َ) on the ل to show how it's pronounced

steps.m: absolutely not! (emphatically negates أُقْسِمُ - I swear)
steps.n: Changed regular alif (ا) to long alif with madda (آ) to add emphasis, making the negation stronger
This is the basic meaning of the root
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Adding أ at start makes it a stronger oath/swearing
This is a demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
Adding بِ at the start shows this is what's being sworn by. This word is connected to أُقْسِمُ (I swear) in this ayah - it's what's being sworn by.
This is the basic meaning of the root
Root letters turned into a place name by adding short 'a' vowels (fatha)

steps.m: the city/town (object of بِ in بِهٰذَا)
steps.n: Adding ٱلْ makes it specific ('the'). The ِ ending shows it's connected to the word بِهٰذَا (by this) in the ayah.

steps.m: you (when speaking to one male person)
steps.n: This is a personal pronoun used to address one male person directly
The و at the start means 'and', connecting this to what came before. This pronoun is the subject of حِلٌّ (free/permissible) in this ayah.

steps.m: to be permissible, to be free (from restrictions)
completely free/unrestricted (describing the Prophet ﷺ in this sacred place)
steps.n: Root letters combined with a shadda (double letter) to make a noun meaning 'completely allowed/permitted'

steps.m: completely free/unrestricted (as the subject describing the Prophet ﷺ)
steps.n: Added double damma to show this is the subject of the sentence
This is a demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby. In this ayah, it's pointing to البَلَدِ (the city/land)
The بِ at the start shows swearing by something or taking an oath by it. In this context, it's Allah swearing by the city/land.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the noun for a settled place

steps.m: the city (object of حِلٌّۢ بِ in this ayah)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific ('the city') and kasra at the end because it follows the word بِ (by/in/with) in حِلٌّۢ بِ (free in)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
This form shows who does the action - like changing 'teach' to 'teacher'
The ٍ shows this is 'a' father (not 'the' father) and that it's being sworn by in this ayah

steps.m: and by a father/parent (being sworn by)
steps.n: The وَ here means 'and' but also helps show this is part of an oath (swearing by something)
This is a particle word that can be used to ask questions or connect ideas
Adding وَ connects this word to وَوَالِدٍ (and a father) that came before it

steps.m: he begat (subject in وَوَالِدٍ وَمَا وَلَدَ - by a father and what he begat)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the past tense verb. This word is part of an oath in the Quran, referring to what a father begets.
This is a basic particle that shows something definitely happened or is true
Added لَ at the beginning to make the emphasis even stronger. This combination appears before خَلَقْنَا (We created) to strongly emphasize that the creation definitely happened.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Adding a sukun (stop mark) at the end makes the word pause
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action. This verb follows لَقَدْ which adds emphasis, like saying 'indeed we created'
The root transforms into a noun pattern
Changes the root into a noun meaning the type of being that is social/familiar with others
Adds ان to create the common word for a single human being
Adds ال to make it specific and َ at the end to show it's being acted upon by the verb 'we created'
This is the basic preposition showing location or position. In this ayah, it shows that mankind is 'in' hardship/struggle.
Added ى at the end to complete the word. This is a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning. The word connects to كَبَدٍ (hardship/struggle) to show mankind is 'in hardship'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form a noun meaning the result of struggling
The tanwin kasratayn (ٍ) shows this word follows the preposition فِى (in) in the ayah
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, changing middle vowels, and adding ُ at end

steps.m: does he think/consider? (questioning what follows: أَن لَّن يَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ أَحَدٌ)
steps.n: Added أَ at start to make it a question. This word is questioning whether someone thinks that no one has power over them.
This is a particle that connects two parts of a sentence. Here it connects 'Does he think' with 'no one will have power over him'
This is a basic negative particle that makes something not happen in the future. In this ayah, it negates يَقْدِرَ (being able to)
Adding the shadda (ّ) makes the meaning stronger, showing complete certainty that something won't happen in the future
Added fathas to connect the root letters and make it past tense
Added يَ at start to make it present tense. Changed vowels to show basic present tense pattern.

steps.m: he would have power (after أَن لَّن in أَيَحْسَبُ أَن لَّن)
steps.n: Changed to subjunctive mood (ending with fatha) because it comes after 'that not' (أَن لَّن) in the question 'Does he think that no one...'
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of or affecting something else
The ى changes to ي before adding pronouns - this is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning
Adding هِ at the end means 'him'. This word connects to يَقْدِرَ (be able), showing who someone won't be able to overcome.
Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make it mean 'one' as a concept or number
Added tanween (double damma) to show this is indefinite. This word is the subject of يَقْدِرَ (be able) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense form
The middle letter و changed to ا which is common for hollow verbs
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense, and changed vowels to show ongoing action
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at the beginning changes the meaning from 'perish' to 'make something perish/destroy'. This verb is acting on مَالًا (wealth) in this ayah.
Adding تُ at the end shows that 'I' did the action. In this ayah, the speaker is talking about wealth (مَالًا) that they destroyed/spent.
Adding the fatha vowel mark helps pronounce the word correctly
Added tanween alif to show this is the object being wasted in the phrase 'I wasted wealth'
Changes to plural noun meaning 'accumulated things'
Makes the word accusative case, showing it's the object in the sentence
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, changing middle vowel to sukun, and adding ُ at end
Added أَ at start to make it a question. This word appears at the start of the ayah, asking if someone thinks/calculates something.
This is a basic connecting word (particle) that joins two parts of a sentence together. Here it connects 'Does he think' (أَيَحْسَبُ) with what comes after it.
Added the fatha mark (َ) above the أ to show how it's pronounced

steps.m: not (changes present verbs to past negative)
steps.n: This is a negative particle that makes verbs negative and changes their time to past. In this ayah, it affects the verb يَرَهُ (see him), making it mean 'did not see him'.

steps.m: not (changes present verbs to past negative)
steps.n: Added emphasis by doubling the ل letter with a shadda. This happens because the word before it ends in a similar sound.
Added vowel marks (fatha) to the root letters to make it mean 'he saw' in the past tense
Added يَ at the beginning to show present tense. The weak letter أ drops out in present tense, and ى appears at the end following Arabic rules for weak letters.
Added هُ at the end to show who is being seen (him). The ى at the end changes to ه when adding pronouns.
Added the small waw (ۥ) after هُ to help with pronunciation when connecting words
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic word
Added tanween damma to show this is the subject doing the action in an indefinite way. This word is the subject of يَرَ (see) in the ayah.
Added مْ to make it negative in the past tense
Added أَ at the beginning to make it a question that expects a 'yes' answer
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

The نَ at the start shows 'we' are doing the action. The sukun on ع and removal of final fatha changes it from past to present tense. This word is part of a question starting with أَلَمْ (did we not?)
Added fatha to show basic preposition meaning
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning
Added هُ to show who the preposition is directed to
Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters together with vowel marks to make the basic noun for 'eye'. This word is what's being given in the ayah.
Added the dual ending to show exactly two eyes. This is what Allah is mentioning as having created for humans in the ayah 'Did We not make for him two eyes?'
Added vowels and alif to make it a noun meaning the physical tongue
Added -an ending to show this is 'a tongue' (the direct object receiving the action)

steps.m: and a tongue (connected to وَشَفَتَيْنِ - and two lips)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with the next word وَشَفَتَيْنِ (and two lips), showing Allah gave humans both a tongue and lips
Added vowel marks and changed the ending to ة to make it mean 'a single lip'
Added ين to show we're talking about two lips instead of one
Added kasra at the end to show it's being used as an object

steps.m: and two lips (connected to وَلِسَانًا - and a tongue)
steps.n: Added و with fatha to connect this word to the previous word وَلِسَانًا (and a tongue)

steps.m: to guide or show the way
steps.n: This root carries the basic meaning of guiding or showing someone the right path
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of a past tense verb.
The ى changes back to يْ when adding more letters after it
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the action
The نَا shortens to نٰ when adding more letters after it
Added هُ at the end to show who received the guidance
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Connected the root letters together with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to form the basic noun
Added ين to show there are exactly two paths
Added ال to make it specific/definite
This is the basic negative particle that means 'no' or 'not'

steps.m: and then not (connects to ٱقْتَحَمَ - he has not attempted)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from a previous idea and connects to what comes next

steps.m: to enter into or tackle something difficult

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: he forcefully tackled/entered into (something that affects ٱلْعَقَبَةَ)
steps.n: Added letters to show he made a strong effort to enter/climb something difficult. Like climbing a steep hill - it takes extra work! Connected to ٱلْعَقَبَةَ (the steep path) in this verse.

steps.m: he forcefully tackled/entered into (something that affects ٱلْعَقَبَةَ)
steps.n: Changed the initial alif to a waṣla form since it comes after فَلَا in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb form
Adding ة at the end turns the verb 'to make difficult' into a noun meaning 'a difficult thing' - like turning 'climb' into 'a steep climb'
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific obstacle, not just any obstacle

steps.m: the steep path/difficult obstacle (object of اقْتَحَمَ/storm/tackle)
steps.n: Added -a ending because it's receiving the action of 'storming/tackling'
This is a question word that asks about something unknown. In this ayah, it's asking about ٱلْعَقَبَةُ (the steep path)
Added و at the beginning to connect this question with what came before it. The آ at the end is just a different way of writing ا that doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'

steps.m: made you know (object of وَمَآ - 'and what')
steps.n: Added كَ to show who is being informed. This word follows وَمَآ (and what) in a question format.
This is the basic interrogative particle used to ask about things. In this ayah, it's asking about what العَقَبَةُ (the steep path) is.
Added fatha mark on م to show correct pronunciation

steps.m: to climb up, ascend, overcome obstacles

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Added ة to make it a noun referring to something that is climbed
Added ٱلْ to make it specific ('the') and ُ to show it's the subject being discussed
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Combined the repeated ك into one letter with shadda to show emphasis, and removed the final vowel to make it a noun
The damma shows this is the subject of an implied verb, meaning 'what is required/commanded is the freeing'. This word connects to رَقَبَةٍ (neck/slave) which specifies what is to be freed.

steps.m: to watch over or guard something
a neck or slave (since these were watched over)
steps.n: Added fatha marks to connect the letters and ة at the end to make it a noun. The meaning shifted from the action of watching to refer to something that is watched over (historically, slaves were closely guarded).
Added the tanween (ٍ) to show this is the object being freed in the phrase فَكُّ رَقَبَةٍ (freeing of a slave)

steps.m: or (connecting two options: either feeding someone or something else)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that gives a choice between options. In this ayah, it connects different ways of doing something good.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed to a noun showing the action itself by adding إِ at the start and changing the middle vowels
Added double dammah to make it indefinite (like saying 'a feeding' instead of 'the feeding')
This is a basic preposition that shows something is inside something else or happens during a specific time. In this ayah, it's used with يَوْمٍ (day) to show when something happens.
Added ى at the end to complete the word. This is a common spelling of this preposition.
Connected the letters with vowel marks to make the basic noun for 'day'

steps.m: a day (after the word في meaning 'in/during')
steps.n: Added two dots under the last letter (ٍ) to show this is 'a day' after the word في (in/during)
This is a special word that shows something has or owns a quality. It connects two words together like saying 'the one with' or 'having' in English.
Added kasra to connect it with the next word that describes what quality or thing is possessed
Added ى at the end to complete the word. In this ayah, it connects to the word مَسْغَبَةٍ (hunger), showing 'having hunger' or 'of hunger'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added مَ at start and changed vowels to show where/when hunger happens
Added ة to make it into an abstract noun showing the state of intense hunger

steps.m: severe hunger (possessed by يَوْمٍ - a day)
steps.n: Added kasratain to show this hunger belongs to or is connected to 'a day' mentioned before it

steps.m: to lack something essential, to be incomplete

steps.n: Forms an adjective describing someone who has lost something essential, especially a parent

steps.m: an orphan (object of feeding in this ayah)
steps.n: Makes the word indefinite and shows it's receiving the action. This orphan is who is being fed in the ayah.
This is a special demonstrative word that points to something. It's like saying 'this' or 'that' in English.
Adding ا (alif) to ذ turns it into 'ذا' meaning 'one who has/possesses'. Like saying 'the one with' something. Here it tells us more about the orphan.
Added vowels to make it into a basic past tense verb
Added مَ at the beginning to show the place or state of being close, and ة at the end to make it into a specific instance or type of closeness
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this describes something owned or connected to something else

steps.m: or (connecting feeding مِسْكِينًا as an alternative)
steps.n: This is a basic connecting word that shows you have a choice between two or more options. In this ayah, it's giving an alternative way to help - feeding مِسْكِينًا (a poor person).

steps.m: to be still or settle down

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Added ي and changed vowels to make a word meaning someone who has this quality (like 'settler' from 'settle')

steps.m: poor person (one made still by need)
steps.n: Added مِ at the start to show someone who experiences poverty and need, like adding '-ed' in English to show something happened to someone
Added ًا to show this is the object being helped in the verse
This is the base form of the demonstrative word used to point to something nearby

steps.m: this one/possessor (describing مِسْكِينًا - a needy person)
steps.n: Adding ا at the end makes it mean 'possessor of' or 'one who has' when used before a noun. Here it describes the needy person mentioned before it.
Added vowels to make the basic verb form. In ancient Arabia, being covered in dust was associated with poverty since the poor often had to sit on the ground.

steps.m: state of poverty (literally: state of being in dust)
steps.n: Added مَ at start and ة at end to create a noun describing the state of being poor/dusty

steps.m: in a state of poverty (literally: in a state of being covered in dust)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this describes someone's state

steps.m: then (connects this ayah to what came before)
steps.n: This word connects events in order, showing that what follows happened after what came before. In this ayah, it connects to the previous description and introduces what comes next.
Added fathas to make it past tense. This is the theoretical form before the weak letter change.
This is a common change with weak letters - و becomes ا when between fathas
Basic preposition showing origin or source
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly to the next word الَّذِينَ (those who). This is like adding glue between words to make them flow better when speaking.
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that refers to someone or something
Added ال with shadda to make it more specific, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'
Added ين at the end to make it plural. This word connects to ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed) in the ayah, showing who did the believing.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added alif after first letter to show this is about actively choosing to believe, not just feeling safe
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. This word is connected to كَانَ (was) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form basic past tense verb
Added و (and) at start + تَ and ا in middle to show people advising each other back and forth

steps.m: and they advised each other (plural past tense)
steps.n: Added plural masculine past tense suffix وا to indicate 'they'
Root letters turned into a simple noun meaning 'patience' by adding vowel marks
Added 'the' to make it specific
Added kasra at the end because it follows the word بِ (with/by)
Added بِ (with/by) at the beginning to show how the patience is used

steps.m: to advise one another, to counsel each other
steps.n: Shows people giving advice back and forth to each other
The ending changed to show this happened in the past tense - they (a group) advised each other

steps.m: and they advised each other (connected to ءَامَنُوا۟)
steps.n: The prefix 'وَ' connects this word to ءَامَنُوا۟ (believed) in the ayah, showing these actions happened together
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changed the verb into a noun that means the quality of showing mercy
Adding ال makes the word definite (like 'the' in English)
The بِ means 'with' or 'by', and the kasra at the end shows it's in the genitive case
This is the basic form of the demonstrative pronoun used to point to things or people.
Adding ئِ changes it to refer to something far away instead of near.
Adding كَ makes it more specific in pointing to something. This word is the subject of أَصْحٰبُ (companions) in this ayah.

steps.m: to be close to someone or something
steps.n: This is the basic meaning showing the idea of companionship
Adding vowels turns the root into a past tense verb meaning 'to accompany'
Changes the word to show many people who stay close to someone or something
Added ُ to show this word is doing something in the sentence. Here it's connected to ٱلْمَيْمَنَةِ (the right side), meaning 'the companions of the right side'

steps.m: to be fortunate, to be on the right side
steps.n: This root relates to blessing and being on the right side

steps.m: he was fortunate/on the right side
steps.n: Connected the letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Added مَ at start and changed middle letter to ي to make it mean 'place of' something
Added ة at the end to change it from a general place to a specific concept meaning 'right side'
Added ٱلْ to make it specific

steps.m: of the right side (possessed by أَصْحٰبُ)
steps.n: Added ِ to show this belongs to the word before it (أَصْحٰبُ - companions), forming 'companions of the right side'
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence
Added ال to make it definite
Changed to plural form by adding ين at the end
Added و (and) at the beginning to connect with previous statement. This word is the subject of كَفَرُوا (they disbelieved) in this ayah.

steps.m: to reject, deny, or cover up truth

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fatha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he rejected/denied'

steps.m: they rejected/denied (referring to those who - الَّذِينَ)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people did this action together. This matches with الَّذِينَ (those who) which comes before it in the ayah.

steps.m: to show, make clear, be evident
a sign (something that shows or proves)
steps.n: Changed the root into a noun that means one specific thing that shows or proves something

steps.m: signs (multiple things that show or prove)
steps.n: Changed from one sign to many signs by adding ات at the end
Added نَا at the end to show these signs belong to Allah
Added بِ at the start to show these signs were the things that were rejected, connecting it to كَفَرُوا (they disbelieved) in the ayah
This is a pronoun that means 'they' or 'them' when talking about a group of men or mixed group. In this ayah, it's connected to أَصْحٰبُ (companions/people) to show who the companions are.

steps.m: to be close to, keep company with

steps.n: Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to accompany'
Changed to active participle form to show who does the action
Changed to broken plural form to show multiple companions
Changed to 'asHaabu' with an 'u' ending to show these companions are doing something, and wrote the long 'a' sound differently

steps.m: to be unlucky or on the left side

steps.n: Root letters were connected with vowels to make past tense verb meaning 'he was unlucky'

steps.m: place/direction of ill fortune, left side
steps.n: Added مَ at start and ة at end to turn the action into a place/direction (like turning 'unlucky' into 'place of bad luck')

steps.m: the place of ill fortune, the left side
steps.n: Added 'the' (ال) at the start and simplified the spelling of hamza

steps.m: of the place of ill fortune/left side (possessed by أَصْحٰبُ)
steps.n: Added kasra to show this word belongs to أَصْحٰبُ (companions/people)
This is a common preposition that shows something is on top of, over, or against something else. In this ayah, it shows what is against/upon them.
هِمْ is added to show it means 'them' (masculine plural)
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is common in Arabic words. The meaning narrows to specifically refer to fire.
Added two dots on top (tanween) to show this is 'a fire' that is being described as closed/sealed in this verse
Added أ (alif) at start and vowels to make 'he' past tense form of 'to close'
Changed to passive participle form to show the state of being closed
Added feminine marker to match the word نَارٌ (fire) which is feminine in Arabic
Added tanween (double damma) to make the word indefinite
